title: Open Workshop: Contracts, Client Oversight Services, Vetting Subs, and Managing Difficult Clients
summary: This open workshop session covered a range of participant-driven topics including contract options for builders (AIA vs. NAHB/GHBA), a homeowner construction oversight consulting business model, subcontractor vetting processes, and strategies for managing difficult or abusive clients on active projects. Erin provided practical frameworks drawn from her own experience and encouraged participants to focus on controlling what they can, staying factual, and setting clear standards early.

teachings.concept: AIA Contracts as a Neutral Standard
teachings.explanation: The American Institute of Architects (AIA) contracts are written to protect all parties fairly, unlike builder association contracts (e.g., NAHB/GHBA) which tend to favor the builder. AIA contracts come in multiple versions for different project types and delivery methods, making them a versatile and trusted standard for both custom builds and remodels.
teachings.quote: The AIA, it just, it looks at the full picture. The GHBA is written, you know, builders pay to get it written, so it's really not that fair for homeowners.

teachings.concept: Homeowner Construction Oversight as a Positioned Business
teachings.explanation: Independent construction oversight consulting—where a third party monitors quality and catches issues during the build—is a recognized and growing service. It is standard practice in some markets and is becoming more necessary as construction quality expectations lag behind rapid market growth. Practitioners must position themselves as being 'on the team' rather than adversarial to contractors.
teachings.quote: When I first started building, I worked for a company called David Weekly Homes, and it was very standard when I started in Austin—it was totally normal for the owner to hire an inspector, an independent inspector, that would come in at rough end stage and make their list.

teachings.concept: Raising Your Hand to Build Awareness for a New Service
teachings.explanation: When offering a service that is not yet well known in a local market, the builder or consultant must proactively educate the public through social media and word of mouth. Simply doing great work and letting clients spread the word is not enough in early stages—active visibility is required.
teachings.quote: You have to put your hand up. Like I do it on social media all the time. You do have to put your hand up and you have to raise your voice a little bit.

teachings.explanation: A single low-ticket service offering like a one-time walkthrough is not a sustainable business model on its own. Consulting businesses need multiple income streams—such as recurring check-ins, ongoing project management, or plan review services—to build a financially viable company.
teachings.quote: If you were to jump into this business as its own business, to me, it would need to have multiple opportunities of income because for $1,500, that's a lot of $1,500 clients.

teachings.explanation: The most reliable way to vet a new subcontractor is to require that they already have working relationships with at least three other trades or vendors in your existing network. This leverages the trust and standards-setting others have already done, reducing your risk when bringing someone new on board.
teachings.quote: That sub needs to have worked with at least three of our other trades. That sub needs to know some of the architects that we work with and the designers we work with. They don't even make the cut until those checkpoints are checked off.

teachings.explanation: Even well-vetted subcontractors will eventually fall short. Builders should expect this and have a plan ready for the moment it happens. Addressing quality issues immediately—even on small items—sets clear standards and determines whether the relationship should continue.
teachings.quote: We have this phrase in our company: they're awesome until they're not, and just expect it. It's just true. They're awesome until they're not. And then you have to have a plan to address that micro moment.

teachings.concept: Providing Visual Control to Difficult Clients
teachings.explanation: When a client relationship is strained and trust has eroded, one of the most effective rebuilding tools is to give clients a visible sense of control and progress. Using physical checklists posted on site, daily top-five task lists, and photo updates communicates that the project is organized and moving forward—calming anxiety without being reactive.
teachings.quote: My first move after breathing and after staying factual would be to visually give a sense of control. When they walk in the home, anyone who sees a checklist—they're like, oh, okay. This is under control.

teachings.concept: Taking Emotion Out and Staying Factual with Difficult People
teachings.explanation: When facing rude or hostile clients, the most effective response is to remove personal emotion from the situation and focus only on facts and the path forward. Reacting emotionally or defensively escalates conflict, while a calm, structured response rebuilds professionalism and trust.
teachings.quote: I take about taking the emotion out of the situation and always dealing with facts. Take the emotion out, take the personalities out. Look at the facts, look at what's going on here.

actionable_advice: Use AIA contracts (available at AIA.org) for both custom builds and remodels instead of builder-association contracts; purchase them as one-offs based on project type and delivery method.

When positioning a construction oversight service, frame it as standard industry practice that exists in many markets and is in the best interest of the project—not as an adversarial check on the contractor.
Use social media to educate your local market about a new or unfamiliar service offering, inviting people to reach out for conversation rather than waiting for demand to develop organically.
Build multiple revenue tiers into a consulting business—such as one-time walkthroughs, recurring check-ins, and full project oversight—to create a sustainable income model.

Before engaging a new subcontractor on a project, require that they have working relationships with at least three trades or vendors already in your network, and walk their current job sites unannounced to evaluate real working conditions.

Address subcontractor quality issues immediately and decisively at the first small mistake—tear out and redo substandard work on the spot to establish non-negotiable standards early in the relationship.

When a client relationship is damaged, create a visible daily checklist (posted on site with a Sharpie) and send photo updates to owners showing completed tasks to rebuild trust through demonstrated progress.
Have a direct meeting with your construction manager to align on standards and accountability before re-engaging a difficult client, rather than making personnel changes reactively.
client_challenges: Tiffany is deciding which contract format to use for her projects and is working with an attorney to create contracts, but is unsure whether AIA, GHBA, or TAB contracts are most appropriate.

Karen is building a homeowner construction oversight consulting business in Miami and is struggling to educate homeowners about the service's value, overcome contractor resistance, and scale beyond a single client at a low price point of $1,500 per walkthrough.
Karen and her partner are uncertain how to position their service so that contractors view them as collaborative team members rather than adversaries.

Alex is vetting new subcontractors who look great on paper but underperform once on the job, and is unsure how to handle disputes about who pays for rework when subs claim deficient work falls within building tolerances.

Erica is dealing with a high-profile but verbally abusive designer client in LA who called her team 'amateur hour' after her construction manager was briefly off-site during inspections, and feels stuck because she cannot exit the project due to financial need.
Erica is concerned that her company may be growing too fast without adequate systems and processes in place, leading to performance gaps and client tension.
Chastity is a new member just getting started and trying to navigate contracts, systems, and processes for a custom home and remodel business launched in late 2024.
notable_quotes: On subcontractor reliability: 'They're awesome until they're not, and just expect it. It's just true. They're awesome until they're not. And then you have to have a plan to address that micro moment.'

On contractor resistance to oversight: 'If there is resistance, I think that's okay. And if that is the case, then the approach would be: this is part of the program. It's it. This is the program. And you'll either make this difficult or not, but we're moving forward.'
On managing difficult clients: 'All they want to know is that things are under control. We're all human—we all want control. So grab the controllables right now.'

On passing inspection not equaling quality: 'The house you're living in passed inspection too, right?' — Karen's team to a resistant contractor, reframing the limits of inspection-only quality assurance.
On referral-based business growth: 'Aaron Stetzer hasn't said a word, but that law firm—they're talking. So for you, if you do a bang up job, that homeowner is going to be sharing your story.'
On self-reflection during conflict: 'When you have a resentment, it's because you had a part in it. What is my part in this? And where am I out of control?'
